metadata.title: AWS Developer Associate DVA-C02 Practice Questions

questions.question: A developer is creating an AWS Lambda function that processes images uploaded to an S3 bucket. Which event source mapping configuration allows the function to be invoked automatically when a new object is created?
options: Configure an S3 event notification with the s3:ObjectCreated:* event type targeting the Lambda function
Create a CloudWatch Events rule that polls the S3 bucket every minute
Use Lambda's built-in S3 polling mechanism by specifying the bucket ARN
Configure an SQS queue to monitor the S3 bucket and trigger Lambda
questions.explanation: S3 event notifications can directly invoke Lambda functions when objects are created, modified, or deleted. The s3:ObjectCreated:* event type covers all creation events (Put, Post, Copy, CompleteMultipartUpload). This is a push-based invocation model where S3 pushes events to Lambda.

questions.question: A developer needs to process messages from an SQS FIFO queue using Lambda. The messages must be processed in order within each message group. What happens if a message in a group fails processing?
options: Lambda continues processing subsequent messages in all message groups without interruption
Lambda stops processing messages in the affected message group but continues processing other groups
Lambda stops processing all messages across all message groups in the queue
Lambda automatically retries the failed message and skips to the next message after three attempts
questions.explanation: With SQS FIFO queues and Lambda, when a message fails in a message group, Lambda pauses processing for that specific message group to maintain ordering guarantees. However, messages in other message groups continue to be processed normally. This ensures per-group ordering while maximizing throughput across groups.

questions.question: A developer is writing a Lambda function that processes DynamoDB Streams. The function needs to handle a scenario where the same stream record might be delivered more than once. Which approach ensures idempotent processing?
options: Enable exactly-once delivery on the DynamoDB Stream
Use the eventID from the stream record as an idempotency key and check a tracking table before processing
Configure the Lambda function with a concurrency limit of 1 to prevent duplicate processing
Set the batch window to 0 seconds to ensure each record is processed immediately
questions.explanation: DynamoDB Streams guarantees at-least-once delivery, meaning records can be delivered more than once. To achieve idempotent processing, use the unique eventID from each stream record as a key and check a tracking table (or use a conditional write) before processing. This ensures that even if a record is delivered multiple times, it is only processed once.

questions.question: A developer needs to invoke a Lambda function synchronously from an API Gateway REST API. Which integration type should be used?
options: AWS_PROXY (Lambda Proxy Integration)
AWS (Lambda Custom Integration) with async invocation
HTTP_PROXY integration pointing to the Lambda function URL
MOCK integration with a Lambda mapping template
questions.explanation: AWS_PROXY (Lambda Proxy Integration) passes the entire API Gateway request to Lambda as a structured event and expects Lambda to return a properly formatted response. This is the most common and simplest integration type for synchronous Lambda invocation from API Gateway, requiring minimal configuration.

questions.question: A developer has a Lambda function with a 15-minute timeout that processes large files. The function occasionally runs out of memory. Which approach should the developer take to handle files that require more processing capacity?
options: Increase the Lambda function's memory allocation, which also proportionally increases CPU power
Increase only the CPU allocation independently of memory in the Lambda configuration
Deploy the function with provisioned concurrency to get more memory per execution
Enable Lambda SnapStart to allocate additional memory during cold starts
questions.explanation: In Lambda, CPU power is allocated proportionally to memory. Increasing memory from 128 MB to 1,769 MB gives one full vCPU. At 10,240 MB (maximum), the function gets 6 vCPUs. There is no way to configure CPU independently of memory. More memory also means more CPU, which can speed up processing and prevent timeouts.

questions.question: A developer needs to create a DynamoDB table that supports queries on both the partition key and an attribute that is not part of the primary key. What should the developer create?
options: A Global Secondary Index (GSI) on the non-key attribute
A Local Secondary Index (LSI) on the non-key attribute
A composite primary key using the non-key attribute as the sort key
A DynamoDB Stream to index the non-key attribute
questions.explanation: A Global Secondary Index (GSI) allows querying on any attribute, regardless of the table's primary key structure. GSIs can be created at any time after table creation and have their own partition key and optional sort key. LSIs can only be created at table creation time and must share the same partition key as the base table.

questions.question: A developer is designing a DynamoDB table for an e-commerce application. The table has a partition key of 'customerId' and a sort key of 'orderId'. The application needs to query all orders for a customer placed within the last 30 days. Which approach is most efficient?
options: Use a Query operation with a key condition expression that filters on customerId and uses a BETWEEN condition on the sort key if orderId encodes the timestamp
Perform a Scan operation with a filter expression on the order date attribute
Create a GSI with orderId as the partition key and use a Scan with a date filter
Use BatchGetItem to retrieve all items for the customer and filter client-side
questions.explanation: The most efficient approach is to design the sort key to include a timestamp prefix (e.g., orderId = '2026-03-15#ORD-1234') and use a Query with a BETWEEN key condition on the sort key. This leverages DynamoDB's native sorting and eliminates the need for filter expressions or scans, which consume more read capacity.

questions.question: A developer is experiencing hot partition issues with a DynamoDB table. The table uses 'deviceId' as the partition key and receives 90% of traffic to 10 specific devices. Which strategy best addresses this problem?
options: Add a random suffix to the partition key to distribute writes (write sharding), then use parallel queries to aggregate results
Increase the provisioned read and write capacity to handle the traffic spike
Create a GSI with a different partition key to redistribute the load
Enable DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) to cache all read requests
questions.explanation: Write sharding adds a random suffix (e.g., deviceId#1 through deviceId#10) to distribute traffic for hot keys across multiple partitions. When reading, the application queries all shard keys in parallel and aggregates the results. While DAX helps with reads, it does not solve write hot partition issues. Increasing capacity does not help because DynamoDB distributes capacity evenly across partitions.

questions.question: A developer needs to store session data that expires automatically after 24 hours. Which DynamoDB feature should be used?
options: Time to Live (TTL) with an attribute containing the expiration epoch timestamp
A CloudWatch Events rule that triggers a Lambda function to delete expired items
A DynamoDB Stream that processes and deletes items older than 24 hours
Conditional deletes using a scheduled batch process
questions.explanation: DynamoDB TTL automatically deletes items after a specified timestamp, at no additional cost. You define a TTL attribute containing an epoch timestamp, and DynamoDB's background process deletes expired items. Note that deletion is not instantaneous - it typically occurs within 48 hours of expiration, but items are filtered from query results once expired.

questions.question: A developer is writing a banking application where a transfer must debit one account and credit another atomically. Which DynamoDB API call ensures both operations succeed or both fail?
options: TransactWriteItems with both Put/Update operations in a single transaction
BatchWriteItem with both operations in the same request
Two separate UpdateItem calls wrapped in application-level retry logic
Use DynamoDB Streams to ensure eventual consistency between the two updates
questions.explanation: TransactWriteItems provides ACID transactions across multiple items and tables. If any operation in the transaction fails, all operations are rolled back. BatchWriteItem does NOT provide atomicity - individual operations can succeed or fail independently. DynamoDB transactions support up to 100 items or 4 MB per transaction.

questions.question: A developer needs to upload a 7 GB file to Amazon S3. Which approach is required?
options: Use multipart upload since the file exceeds the 5 GB single PUT limit
Use a single PUT request with the Transfer-Encoding: chunked header
Compress the file to under 5 GB and use a single PUT request
Use S3 Transfer Acceleration with a single PUT request
questions.explanation: Amazon S3 has a 5 GB limit for single PUT object operations. For files larger than 5 GB, multipart upload is required. AWS recommends using multipart upload for files larger than 100 MB. Multipart upload allows parallel uploads of parts (5 MB to 5 GB each), improving throughput and providing the ability to resume failed uploads.

questions.question: A developer's application allows users to upload files directly to S3 from a web browser without routing through the application server. Which approach should the developer implement?
options: Generate a presigned POST URL on the server and have the browser upload directly to S3 using the presigned URL
Embed the AWS access key and secret key in the JavaScript code for direct S3 access
Create a public S3 bucket and allow direct browser uploads without authentication
Use S3 Transfer Acceleration endpoint in the browser with an IAM user's credentials
questions.explanation: Presigned URLs (or presigned POST policies) allow temporary, secure access to upload objects to S3 without exposing AWS credentials. The server generates the presigned URL with specific conditions (file size, content type, expiration) and returns it to the browser. The browser then uploads directly to S3 using the presigned URL, reducing server load.

questions.question: A developer is implementing an S3 event-driven architecture. When an object is uploaded, it must be processed by three different Lambda functions in parallel. What is the recommended approach?
options: Configure the S3 event notification to publish to an SNS topic, then subscribe all three Lambda functions to that topic
Configure three separate S3 event notifications on the same bucket, each targeting a different Lambda function for the same event type
Use S3 Batch Operations to invoke all three Lambda functions after upload
Configure S3 Replication to copy the object to three different buckets, each with its own Lambda trigger
questions.explanation: S3 allows only one event notification configuration per event type per prefix/suffix combination. To fan out to multiple Lambda functions, publish the S3 event to an SNS topic, then subscribe each Lambda function to that topic. This is the SNS fan-out pattern. Alternatively, you could use S3 Event Notifications to EventBridge, which supports multiple targets per event.

questions.question: A developer needs to ensure that only the latest version of an object is accessible in an S3 bucket, but wants the ability to recover accidentally deleted objects. Which S3 feature combination should be configured?
options: Enable S3 Versioning on the bucket to retain all versions and recover deleted objects via delete markers
Enable S3 Object Lock in Governance mode to prevent accidental deletion
Configure S3 Lifecycle rules to move deleted objects to Glacier
Enable Cross-Region Replication as a backup for deleted objects
questions.explanation: S3 Versioning retains all versions of an object. When an object is deleted, S3 adds a delete marker instead of permanently removing it. The latest version appears deleted, but previous versions can be restored by removing the delete marker. This provides protection against both accidental overwrites and deletions without requiring additional services.

questions.question: A developer is building a decoupled architecture where a producer service sends messages and a consumer service processes them asynchronously. Which AWS service is most appropriate for this point-to-point messaging pattern?
options: Amazon SQS (Simple Queue Service)
Amazon SNS (Simple Notification Service)
Amazon EventBridge
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
questions.explanation: Amazon SQS is a fully managed message queuing service designed for point-to-point messaging. A producer sends messages to a queue, and a consumer polls and processes them. SQS ensures each message is delivered at least once and processed by one consumer. SNS is for pub/sub (one-to-many) patterns, not point-to-point.

questions.question: A developer needs to implement a message ordering guarantee where messages with the same order ID are processed in sequence, but messages with different order IDs can be processed in parallel. Which configuration should be used?
options: Use an SQS FIFO queue with the order ID as the MessageGroupId
Use an SQS Standard queue with the order ID in the message body and sort client-side
Use an SNS FIFO topic with message deduplication enabled
Use Amazon Kinesis with the order ID as the partition key
questions.explanation: SQS FIFO queues guarantee ordering within a MessageGroupId. Messages with the same MessageGroupId are processed in strict FIFO order, while messages with different MessageGroupIds can be processed in parallel. This provides both ordering guarantees and high throughput. FIFO queues support up to 3,000 messages per second with batching.

questions.question: A developer notices that some messages in an SQS queue are being processed more than once, causing duplicate orders. The application cannot be modified to be idempotent. Which solution prevents duplicate message delivery?
options: Migrate to an SQS FIFO queue with content-based deduplication enabled
Reduce the visibility timeout to the minimum value of 0 seconds
Increase the maximum receives setting on the dead-letter queue redrive policy
Enable long polling with a wait time of 20 seconds
questions.explanation: SQS FIFO queues with content-based deduplication use a SHA-256 hash of the message body to detect and reject duplicate messages within a 5-minute deduplication interval. Standard queues provide at-least-once delivery, meaning duplicates can occur. FIFO queues provide exactly-once processing, preventing duplicates. Note the throughput limit of 300 messages/sec (3,000 with batching).

questions.question: A developer needs to send a notification to multiple subscribers (email, SMS, and a Lambda function) when an event occurs. Which AWS service enables this pub/sub pattern?
options: Amazon SNS (Simple Notification Service)
Amazon SQS (Simple Queue Service)
Amazon EventBridge
AWS Step Functions
questions.explanation: Amazon SNS implements the publish/subscribe pattern where a message published to an SNS topic is delivered to all subscribers simultaneously. SNS supports multiple subscription protocols including email, SMS, HTTP/HTTPS, SQS, Lambda, and mobile push notifications. This is a one-to-many messaging pattern.

questions.question: A developer is building an order processing workflow with the following steps: validate order, charge payment, reserve inventory, and send confirmation. If the payment charge fails, previous steps must be reversed. Which AWS service best implements this pattern?
options: AWS Step Functions with a Saga pattern using compensation steps for each action
Amazon SQS with separate queues for each step and dead-letter queues for failures
AWS Lambda with nested function invocations and try/catch blocks
Amazon EventBridge with rules that trigger compensating actions on failure events
questions.explanation: AWS Step Functions is ideal for implementing the Saga pattern, where each step has a compensating action (rollback). Step Functions provides built-in error handling, retry logic, and the ability to define compensating transactions in Catch blocks. If a step fails, the workflow can execute compensation steps in reverse order to maintain data consistency.

questions.question: A developer is building a Step Functions workflow that orchestrates multiple Lambda functions. One Lambda function takes 20 minutes to complete. Which Step Functions workflow type should be used, and what is the concern?
options: Use Standard Workflows since Express Workflows have a 5-minute maximum duration
Use Express Workflows with an increased timeout configuration
Use Standard Workflows with synchronous Express sub-workflows for each step
Use Express Workflows with a wait state to extend the timeout
questions.explanation: Express Workflows have a maximum duration of 5 minutes and are designed for high-volume, short-duration workloads. Standard Workflows support execution durations up to one year, making them suitable for long-running processes. However, note that Lambda itself has a 15-minute maximum timeout, so a 20-minute Lambda execution is not possible - the workflow design would need to be adjusted.

questions.question: A developer needs to build an API that allows clients to manage a list of to-do items with CRUD operations. Which API Gateway API type is the simplest and most cost-effective option?
options: HTTP API (API Gateway v2) for simple REST-like APIs with lower cost
REST API (API Gateway v1) for full-featured REST APIs
WebSocket API for real-time bidirectional communication
GraphQL API using AWS AppSync
questions.explanation: HTTP APIs (API Gateway v2) are designed for simple APIs and are up to 71% cheaper than REST APIs. They support Lambda proxy integration, OIDC/OAuth 2.0 authorization, and CORS configuration. REST APIs offer more features (API keys, request validation, caching, WAF integration) but at higher cost. For simple CRUD operations, HTTP APIs are the recommended choice.

questions.question: A developer deploys a Lambda function with 512 MB of memory. The function processes requests in 3 seconds. After increasing memory to 1,024 MB, the function processes requests in 1.5 seconds. Assuming 1 million invocations per month, which configuration is more cost-effective?
options: The 1,024 MB configuration because the reduced duration offsets the higher per-ms memory cost
The 512 MB configuration because lower memory always means lower cost
Both configurations cost the same because Lambda bills per invocation, not per duration
The 1,024 MB configuration only if provisioned concurrency is enabled
questions.explanation: Lambda charges based on (memory allocated) x (duration in ms). At 512 MB for 3 seconds: 512 * 3000 = 1,536,000 MB-ms per invocation. At 1,024 MB for 1.5 seconds: 1024 * 1500 = 1,536,000 MB-ms per invocation. In this case, costs are identical, but in practice the higher-memory function often costs less because CPU-bound tasks scale better than linearly with memory increases.

questions.question: A developer is building a leaderboard application using DynamoDB. The table has 'gameId' as the partition key and 'score' as the sort key. The application needs to display the top 10 scores across ALL games. Which approach is most efficient?
options: Create a GSI with a fixed partition key (e.g., 'ALL') and 'score' as the sort key, then Query the GSI with ScanIndexForward=false and Limit=10
Perform a Scan operation on the base table with a Limit of 10
Use a PartiQL SELECT statement with ORDER BY score DESC LIMIT 10
Create a GSI with 'score' as the partition key and Query for each possible score value
questions.explanation: By creating a GSI with a fixed partition key (e.g., 'GLOBAL_LEADERBOARD') and 'score' as the sort key, all items map to a single GSI partition sorted by score. Setting ScanIndexForward=false returns items in descending order, and Limit=10 returns only the top 10. A Scan would read the entire table and is inefficient. Note: this pattern creates a hot partition, which works for read-heavy leaderboards but may need write sharding at scale.

questions.question: A developer needs to store objects in S3 with automatic encryption at rest without managing encryption keys. Which is the simplest approach?
options: Use SSE-S3 (Server-Side Encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys)
Use SSE-KMS with a customer managed key (CMK)
Use SSE-C (Server-Side Encryption with Customer-Provided Keys)
Use client-side encryption with the AWS Encryption SDK
questions.explanation: SSE-S3 is the simplest encryption option. Amazon S3 manages the encryption keys entirely, requiring no additional configuration beyond enabling default encryption on the bucket. Each object is encrypted with a unique key, and the key itself is encrypted by a root key that S3 regularly rotates. SSE-S3 is now the default encryption for all new S3 buckets.

questions.question: A developer has configured a Lambda function with an SQS queue as the event source. Messages are failing and being retried, eventually ending up in a dead-letter queue. The developer wants failed messages to be retried exactly 3 times before moving to the DLQ. How should this be configured?
options: Set maxReceiveCount to 3 in the SQS queue's redrive policy for the dead-letter queue
Set the Lambda function's retry attempts to 3 in the event source mapping
Configure the Lambda function's asynchronous invocation retry policy to 3 attempts
Set the SQS queue's visibility timeout to 3 times the Lambda function timeout
questions.explanation: When Lambda is triggered by an SQS queue, failed messages return to the queue after the visibility timeout expires. The maxReceiveCount in the SQS redrive policy controls how many times a message can be received before being sent to the DLQ. Setting maxReceiveCount to 3 means the message is tried 3 times before being redirected. The Lambda async retry configuration only applies to asynchronous invocations, not SQS event source mappings.

questions.question: A developer is implementing a Step Functions workflow that must call an external REST API and wait for a callback. The external system will notify completion by calling back to Step Functions. Which task integration pattern should be used?
options: Use the .waitForTaskToken integration pattern by passing a task token to the external system in the API call
Use the .sync integration pattern to poll the external API for completion
Use a Wait state with a fixed delay estimated to match the external API processing time
Use an Express Workflow with a Lambda function that polls the external API in a loop
questions.explanation: The .waitForTaskToken pattern pauses the Step Functions execution and generates a unique task token. This token is passed to the external system (e.g., in the API call body). When the external system completes processing, it calls SendTaskSuccess or SendTaskFailure with the task token to resume the workflow. This is ideal for human approval workflows or external system callbacks.

questions.question: A developer needs to share common code (utility functions and libraries) across multiple Lambda functions without duplicating the code in each deployment package. Which Lambda feature should be used?
options: Lambda Layers
Lambda Extensions
Lambda Versions
Lambda Aliases
questions.explanation: Lambda Layers allow developers to package libraries, custom runtimes, and other dependencies separately from function code. A layer is a ZIP archive that gets extracted to /opt in the execution environment. Multiple functions can reference the same layer, reducing deployment package size and enabling code reuse. A function can use up to 5 layers.

questions.question: A developer wants to reduce Lambda cold start times for a Java-based function. Which feature initializes a snapshot of the execution environment at deployment time?
options: Lambda SnapStart
Provisioned Concurrency
Lambda Layers with pre-compiled dependencies
Lambda Extensions with initialization hooks
questions.explanation: Lambda SnapStart takes a snapshot of the initialized execution environment (after the init phase) at deployment time using Firecracker microVM snapshots. When the function is invoked, Lambda restores from the snapshot instead of running the full initialization, reducing cold start times by up to 10x. SnapStart is available for Java 11+ runtimes. Provisioned Concurrency also eliminates cold starts but costs more.

questions.question: A developer needs to implement a counter in DynamoDB that increments atomically, even under high concurrency. Multiple processes may increment the counter simultaneously. Which approach is correct?
options: Use UpdateItem with SET counter = counter + :val using an atomic counter (ADD expression)
Read the current value with GetItem, increment in application code, then PutItem with the new value
Use TransactWriteItems with a condition expression to check the current value before updating
Use BatchWriteItem to update the counter with the new value
questions.explanation: DynamoDB supports atomic counters using the UpdateItem operation with ADD (or SET with + operator). The update is atomic at the database level, meaning concurrent updates do not overwrite each other. Each UpdateItem atomically reads and increments the value. The read-modify-write pattern (option B) is not safe under concurrency. TransactWriteItems adds unnecessary overhead for a simple counter.

questions.question: A developer needs to retrieve only specific attributes from S3 objects stored in CSV format without downloading the entire object. Which S3 feature should be used?
options: S3 Select to query the object using SQL expressions
S3 Inventory to catalog and filter objects
S3 Access Points with data filtering policies
S3 Object Lambda to transform the response
questions.explanation: S3 Select allows you to use SQL expressions (SELECT, WHERE, LIMIT) to retrieve only a subset of data from objects stored in CSV, JSON, or Parquet formats. This reduces data transfer and processing time because only the matching data is returned rather than the entire object. S3 Select can reduce transfer costs by up to 80% for suitable workloads.

questions.question: A developer wants to process messages from an SQS queue but only make a request to the queue when messages are likely available, reducing the number of empty responses. Which polling approach should be used?
options: Long polling with WaitTimeSeconds set to a value between 1 and 20 seconds
Short polling with a 1-second delay between requests
Enable the queue's notification feature to push messages to the consumer
Use SQS FIFO queues which only return messages when available
questions.explanation: Long polling reduces empty responses and costs by allowing SQS to wait up to 20 seconds for a message to become available before returning an empty response. Short polling returns immediately even if no messages are available, resulting in many empty responses. Long polling is enabled by setting WaitTimeSeconds (1-20) on ReceiveMessage calls or as a queue attribute.

questions.question: A developer is using API Gateway with a REST API. The API must transform the incoming JSON request body into a different format before passing it to the backend Lambda function. Which feature should be used?
options: A mapping template in the integration request using Velocity Template Language (VTL)
A Lambda authorizer that transforms the request body
A usage plan with request transformation rules
An API Gateway stage variable that defines the transformation
questions.explanation: API Gateway mapping templates use Apache Velocity Template Language (VTL) to transform request and response payloads. These are configured in the Integration Request (for request transformation) and Integration Response (for response transformation). Mapping templates are only available with AWS (custom) integration, not with Lambda Proxy integration which passes the entire request to Lambda.

questions.question: A developer has a Lambda function triggered by an SQS queue with a batch size of 10. If the function fails while processing a batch, what happens to the messages in the batch?
options: All 10 messages become visible again after the visibility timeout and are retried, unless the function uses partial batch responses
Only the failed messages are returned to the queue; successfully processed messages are deleted
All 10 messages are immediately sent to the dead-letter queue
The Lambda service automatically identifies and retries only the failed messages
questions.explanation: By default, when a Lambda function fails while processing an SQS batch, ALL messages in the batch become visible again after the visibility timeout expires. This means even successfully processed messages will be reprocessed. To avoid this, enable ReportBatchItemFailures in the event source mapping, and return the failed message IDs in the function's response. This feature is called partial batch responses.

questions.question: A developer creates a DynamoDB table and needs to quickly read many items by their primary keys. Which API operation allows efficient batch reads?
options: BatchGetItem to retrieve up to 100 items across one or more tables in a single request
Query with a list of key values in a filter expression
Scan with a filter expression matching the desired keys
GetItem called in a loop for each key
questions.explanation: BatchGetItem retrieves up to 100 items (or 16 MB) in a single request, potentially from multiple tables. Each item is retrieved by its full primary key. This is much more efficient than calling GetItem in a loop because it reduces the number of network round trips. Any unprocessed keys are returned in the response for retry.

questions.question: A developer is designing a system where order events must be processed exactly once and in order, with a throughput requirement of 50,000 messages per second. Which service and configuration should be used?
options: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with the order ID as the partition key and the Kinesis Client Library (KCL) for processing
Amazon SQS FIFO queue with message deduplication enabled
Amazon SNS FIFO topic with an SQS FIFO queue subscriber
Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka) with ordered partitions
questions.explanation: SQS FIFO queues are limited to 3,000 messages per second with batching (300 without), which is insufficient for 50,000 msg/sec. Kinesis Data Streams can handle this throughput by adding enough shards (each shard supports 1,000 writes/sec and 2 MB/sec). Using the order ID as the partition key ensures ordering per order. KCL manages shard processing and checkpointing. At 50 shards, Kinesis handles 50,000 messages/sec.

questions.question: A developer needs to create a REST API that returns a list of products from a DynamoDB table. The API should cache responses for 5 minutes to reduce DynamoDB reads. Where should caching be configured?
options: Enable API Gateway stage-level caching with a TTL of 300 seconds
Add a CloudFront distribution in front of the API Gateway
Implement caching logic inside the Lambda function using global variables
Enable DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) for the DynamoDB table
questions.explanation: API Gateway provides built-in caching at the stage level. When enabled, API Gateway caches responses from the backend for a specified TTL (0-3600 seconds). Subsequent identical requests are served from the cache without invoking the backend. This reduces both latency and backend load. Cache capacity ranges from 0.5 GB to 237 GB. DAX also provides caching but at the database layer rather than the API layer.

questions.question: A developer needs to run a Lambda function inside a VPC to access an RDS database. After deploying the function, it can access RDS but cannot access the internet to call a third-party API. What is the issue?
options: The Lambda function needs a NAT Gateway in a public subnet, with the function deployed in a private subnet that routes internet traffic through the NAT Gateway
The Lambda function's security group needs an outbound rule allowing all traffic to 0.0.0.0/0
The Lambda function needs an Elastic IP assigned for internet access
Internet access requires the Lambda function to be in a public subnet with a public IP
questions.explanation: Lambda functions in a VPC are placed in private subnets using Elastic Network Interfaces (ENIs). Private subnets do not have direct internet access. To enable internet access, configure a NAT Gateway in a public subnet and update the private subnet's route table to send internet-bound traffic (0.0.0.0/0) through the NAT Gateway. Lambda functions cannot use public IPs directly.

questions.question: A developer needs to perform a DynamoDB Query that returns items where an attribute matches a specific value, but the attribute is not part of the key schema. Where should this condition be placed?
options: In a FilterExpression that is applied after the Query reads matching items from the table
In the KeyConditionExpression alongside the partition and sort key conditions
In a ProjectionExpression to filter the returned attributes
In a ConditionExpression that prevents the query from reading non-matching items
questions.explanation: FilterExpression is applied after the Query operation reads items matching the key conditions, but before results are returned to the client. It filters out items that do not match the non-key attribute condition. Important: FilterExpression does NOT reduce the read capacity consumed - DynamoDB still reads all items matching the key conditions. Only the results returned to the client are filtered.

questions.question: A developer needs to configure S3 bucket notifications to trigger different Lambda functions based on the object key prefix. Objects under 'images/' should trigger image processing, and objects under 'documents/' should trigger document processing. How should this be configured?
options: Create two separate S3 event notification configurations with different prefix filters, each targeting its respective Lambda function
Create one event notification that sends to an SNS topic, then use subscription filter policies to route to different Lambda functions
Create one Lambda function that checks the key prefix and invokes the appropriate processing function
This is not possible; S3 only supports one event notification per event type per bucket
questions.explanation: S3 supports multiple event notification configurations on the same bucket as long as they have non-overlapping prefix and suffix filter combinations. You can configure 'images/*' to trigger one Lambda function and 'documents/*' to trigger another for the same event type (e.g., s3:ObjectCreated:*). The filters ensure that each configuration applies to a distinct set of objects.

questions.question: A developer is building a Step Functions workflow that needs to process 1,000 items in parallel, with a maximum of 40 concurrent executions. Which Step Functions state should be used?
options: Map state with MaxConcurrency set to 40
Parallel state with 40 branches, each processing 25 items
Choice state with 40 parallel branches based on item index
Task state in a loop with a concurrency semaphore
questions.explanation: The Map state iterates over an array of items and processes each item using a set of steps. The MaxConcurrency parameter limits the number of concurrent iterations. Setting MaxConcurrency to 40 processes up to 40 items simultaneously from the 1,000-item array. The Parallel state is for a fixed number of different branches, not for iterating over a dynamic collection.

questions.question: A developer wants to set environment variables for a Lambda function that differ between development and production. What is the recommended approach?
options: Configure environment variables in the Lambda function configuration, using different values per alias or version
Hardcode the environment-specific values in the function code with conditional logic
Store the values in an S3 bucket and read them at runtime
Use Lambda Layers to store environment-specific configuration
questions.explanation: Lambda environment variables are key-value pairs configured in the function settings. They are available to the function code via standard environment variable access (e.g., os.environ in Python, process.env in Node.js). Different aliases or versions can have different environment variable values, enabling environment-specific configuration without code changes.

questions.question: A developer is using DynamoDB and needs to ensure a PutItem operation only succeeds if an item with the same primary key does NOT already exist. Which approach prevents overwriting existing items?
options: Add a ConditionExpression of 'attribute_not_exists(pk)' to the PutItem operation
Use the 'Expected' parameter with 'Exists: false' in the PutItem operation
Check with GetItem first, then call PutItem if the item does not exist
Use BatchWriteItem with a conditional flag to prevent overwrites
questions.explanation: The ConditionExpression 'attribute_not_exists(pk)' (where pk is the partition key attribute name) ensures the PutItem only succeeds if no item with that primary key exists. If the item already exists, a ConditionalCheckFailedException is thrown. This is an atomic operation - there is no race condition between the check and the write. The GetItem-then-PutItem approach has a race condition.

questions.question: A developer receives a 'ProvisionedThroughputExceededException' error when writing to a DynamoDB table. The application is using the AWS SDK. What is the first action the developer should take?
options: Verify the SDK is using exponential backoff with jitter for retries, which is the default behavior
Immediately increase the table's provisioned write capacity
Switch to DynamoDB On-Demand mode
Implement application-level queuing to throttle writes
questions.explanation: The AWS SDK includes automatic retry with exponential backoff for throttled requests. The developer should first verify this default behavior is not overridden, as most transient throttling resolves with retries. If throttling persists, investigate the access pattern for hot partitions before increasing capacity, since adding more capacity does not help if traffic is concentrated on a single partition.

questions.question: A developer is using API Gateway and needs different API configurations for development and production environments. Which API Gateway feature provides this separation?
options: Stages (e.g., dev, staging, prod) with stage variables for environment-specific configuration
Separate API Gateway instances for each environment
Usage plans with different API keys per environment
Resource policies that restrict access by environment
questions.explanation: API Gateway stages represent different deployment environments (dev, staging, prod) within the same API. Each stage has its own invoke URL, stage variables (for environment-specific configuration), and settings (throttling, caching, logging). Stage variables can be used in integration endpoints, mapping templates, and Lambda function ARNs to point to different backends.

questions.question: A developer has a Lambda function with reserved concurrency set to 100. During a traffic spike, some invocations return a 429 (TooManyRequestsException) error. The function's maximum concurrency metrics show it reached 100. What should the developer do?
options: Increase the reserved concurrency limit or remove it to allow the function to use the account's unreserved concurrency pool
Enable provisioned concurrency at 100 to handle the traffic spike
Add an SQS queue between the caller and Lambda to buffer requests
Increase the Lambda function's memory to process requests faster
questions.explanation: Reserved concurrency sets a hard limit on the maximum number of concurrent executions for a function. When this limit is reached, additional invocations are throttled (429 error). To handle more traffic, increase the reserved concurrency or remove the reservation to use unreserved account concurrency. Provisioned concurrency pre-warms instances but does not increase the maximum concurrent executions beyond the reserved limit.

questions.question: A developer needs to query a DynamoDB table for items where the sort key begins with a specific prefix. Which operator should be used in the KeyConditionExpression?
options: begins_with(sortKey, :prefix)
contains(sortKey, :prefix)
sortKey LIKE ':prefix%'
startsWith(sortKey, :prefix)
questions.explanation: The begins_with() function is supported in DynamoDB's KeyConditionExpression for sort key conditions. It efficiently queries items where the sort key starts with the specified prefix, leveraging the B-tree index structure. The contains() function is only available in FilterExpression, not KeyConditionExpression. DynamoDB does not support SQL-like LIKE operators.

questions.question: A developer needs to host a static website on Amazon S3. Which configuration is required to serve the website content?
options: Enable static website hosting on the bucket and configure an index document
Create an API Gateway REST API that proxies requests to S3
Deploy a Lambda function that reads and serves files from S3
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration to serve content faster
questions.explanation: S3 static website hosting serves HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and other static files directly from an S3 bucket. Configuration requires enabling static website hosting and specifying an index document (e.g., index.html). The website is accessed via the S3 website endpoint (bucket-name.s3-website-region.amazonaws.com). For HTTPS and custom domains, add a CloudFront distribution.

questions.question: A developer is building a system that publishes events to SNS. Some subscribers need all events, while others should only receive events matching specific criteria. How should this be implemented?
options: Use SNS message filtering with subscription filter policies to deliver only matching messages to each subscriber
Create separate SNS topics for each event type and publish to the appropriate topic
Have each subscriber receive all messages and filter them client-side
Use SNS message attributes and Lambda functions as intermediaries to route messages
questions.explanation: SNS subscription filter policies allow each subscriber to define conditions for which messages it receives, based on message attributes. SNS evaluates the filter policy against message attributes and only delivers matching messages. This eliminates the need for multiple topics or client-side filtering. Filter policies support exact match, prefix match, numeric range, and exists/not-exists operators.

questions.question: A developer needs to grant a Lambda function temporary credentials to access a DynamoDB table. What is the recommended approach?
options: Create an IAM execution role with a policy granting DynamoDB access and attach it to the Lambda function
Embed IAM access keys in the Lambda function's environment variables
Create an IAM user with DynamoDB permissions and hardcode the credentials in the function code
Use the root account credentials in the Lambda function
questions.explanation: Lambda execution roles provide temporary security credentials via AWS STS that are automatically rotated. The role's policy defines what AWS services the function can access. Lambda assumes this role on each invocation, and the temporary credentials are available via the AWS SDK. Never embed long-term credentials (access keys) in function code or environment variables.

questions.question: A developer needs to allow a Lambda function in Account A to read from an S3 bucket in Account B. Which approach requires the LEAST configuration?
options: Add a resource-based policy (bucket policy) on the S3 bucket in Account B that grants s3:GetObject to Account A's Lambda execution role ARN
Create a cross-account IAM role in Account B and modify the Lambda function to assume that role
Set up VPC peering between the two accounts and use VPC endpoints
Configure AWS Organizations with a shared services OU
questions.explanation: A resource-based policy on the S3 bucket in Account B can directly grant permissions to the Lambda execution role in Account A without requiring the function to assume a separate role. The bucket policy specifies the cross-account principal ARN. This is simpler than cross-account role assumption, which requires modifying both the role's trust policy and the Lambda function code.

questions.question: A developer is implementing fine-grained access control for a DynamoDB table. Each user should only be able to read and write their own items, identified by a 'userId' partition key that matches their Cognito identity. Which IAM policy condition achieves this?
options: Use a policy condition key 'dynamodb:LeadingKeys' with '${cognito-identity.amazonaws.com:sub}' to restrict access to items where the partition key matches the user's Cognito identity
Create a separate DynamoDB table for each user with an IAM policy scoped to their table
Use a VPC endpoint policy to restrict DynamoDB access by user identity
Implement row-level security in a Lambda function that validates the userId before each query
questions.explanation: The 'dynamodb:LeadingKeys' condition key restricts access to items based on the partition key value. Combined with the Cognito identity variable '${cognito-identity.amazonaws.com:sub}', it ensures users can only access items where the partition key matches their unique Cognito identity. This is fine-grained access control at the IAM level without application-level checks.

questions.question: A developer needs to encrypt data before storing it in DynamoDB. The encryption must use a customer-managed key that the developer's organization controls. Which service should be used?
options: AWS KMS with a customer managed key (CMK) configured for DynamoDB encryption at rest
AWS CloudHSM to generate and store the encryption key
AWS Secrets Manager to store the encryption key
S3 server-side encryption with a customer-provided key
questions.explanation: DynamoDB encryption at rest can be configured with a customer managed AWS KMS key (CMK). This gives the organization full control over the key lifecycle, including creation, rotation, disabling, and access policies. KMS integrates natively with DynamoDB, so the encryption/decryption is transparent to the application. AWS owned keys and AWS managed keys are the other options but don't provide customer control.

questions.question: A developer is implementing envelope encryption for large data objects. Which is the correct sequence of operations?
options: Call KMS GenerateDataKey to get a plaintext data key and encrypted data key, encrypt the data with the plaintext key, store the encrypted data and encrypted data key together, then discard the plaintext key
Call KMS Encrypt with the full data object to encrypt it directly
Generate a random key locally, encrypt the data, then call KMS Encrypt on the random key
Call KMS GenerateRandom to create a key, encrypt the data, then store the key in Secrets Manager
questions.explanation: Envelope encryption uses KMS to generate a data key (GenerateDataKey). KMS returns both a plaintext copy (for encryption) and an encrypted copy (for storage). The plaintext key encrypts the data locally, then is discarded. The encrypted data key is stored alongside the encrypted data. To decrypt, KMS decrypts the data key, which then decrypts the data. This avoids sending large data to KMS (4 KB limit on direct encryption).

questions.question: A developer's application calls KMS Decrypt thousands of times per second and is being throttled. Which approach reduces KMS API calls while maintaining security?
options: Cache the plaintext data keys locally and reuse them for multiple encryption/decryption operations within a defined time period
Increase the KMS API rate limit by contacting AWS Support
Switch from KMS to client-side encryption without KMS
Use KMS multi-region keys to distribute the load across regions
questions.explanation: The AWS Encryption SDK and data key caching feature allow caching of plaintext data keys in memory. A cached key can be reused for multiple encrypt/decrypt operations, dramatically reducing KMS API calls. You configure the cache with a maximum age, maximum bytes encrypted, and maximum number of messages. This approach maintains security while staying within KMS rate limits (5,500-30,000 requests/second depending on region).

questions.question: A developer needs to store database connection strings and API keys securely for use in Lambda functions. Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?
options: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store (SecureString type) or AWS Secrets Manager
Lambda environment variables without encryption
An S3 bucket with restricted access
A DynamoDB table with IAM-restricted access
questions.explanation: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store (SecureString parameters) and AWS Secrets Manager are both designed for secure credential storage. SecureString parameters are encrypted with KMS. Secrets Manager additionally provides automatic rotation, cross-account sharing, and native RDS/Redshift/DocumentDB credential rotation. Lambda functions retrieve secrets at runtime using the SDK.

questions.question: A developer needs to store API keys in Parameter Store. The keys must be encrypted at rest and have access audited. Which Parameter Store configuration should be used?
options: SecureString parameter type with a KMS key, and enable CloudTrail to log Parameter Store API calls
String parameter type with the value base64-encoded for security
StringList parameter type with IAM policies restricting access
SecureString parameter type stored in the Advanced tier for audit logging
questions.explanation: SecureString parameters are encrypted using KMS keys (either the default AWS-managed key or a customer-managed key). CloudTrail automatically logs Parameter Store API calls including GetParameter and PutParameter, providing an audit trail of who accessed which parameters and when. The Standard tier supports SecureString; the Advanced tier is for parameters larger than 4 KB or when using parameter policies.

questions.question: A developer has Lambda functions that need database credentials which must be automatically rotated every 30 days. The rotation must not cause any downtime. Which approach meets these requirements?
options: Use AWS Secrets Manager with automatic rotation enabled, using the alternating users rotation strategy with two sets of credentials
Store credentials in Parameter Store with a Lambda function that rotates them on a schedule
Use IAM database authentication instead of password-based credentials
Store credentials in environment variables and redeploy Lambda functions monthly with new values
questions.explanation: Secrets Manager's alternating users strategy maintains two sets of credentials. During rotation, it creates new credentials in the 'pending' slot while the current credentials remain active. Once the new credentials are verified, they become 'current' and the old credentials become 'previous'. This ensures zero-downtime rotation because at least one set of valid credentials is always available. Lambda functions cache and refresh credentials automatically.

questions.question: A developer creates an IAM policy with the following statement: {"Effect": "Allow", "Action": "s3:GetObject", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*"}. What does the '/*' at the end of the resource ARN signify?
options: The policy applies to all objects within the bucket, not the bucket itself
The policy applies to the bucket and all objects within it
The policy applies only to the root-level objects, not objects in subfolders
The '/*' is optional and has no effect on the policy
questions.explanation: In S3 ARNs, 'arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket' refers to the bucket itself (for actions like ListBucket), while 'arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*' refers to all objects within the bucket (for actions like GetObject, PutObject). The '/*' wildcard is essential for object-level operations. Without it, the GetObject action would not match any objects, and the policy would be ineffective.

questions.question: A developer has both an identity-based policy (Allow s3:*) and a bucket policy (Deny s3:PutObject) applied. What is the effective permission for PutObject?
options: Deny, because an explicit Deny in any policy always overrides any Allow
Allow, because identity-based policies take precedence over resource-based policies
Allow, because the Allow is more specific with s3:*
It depends on which policy was created first
questions.explanation: In IAM policy evaluation, an explicit Deny in ANY policy (identity-based, resource-based, SCP, permissions boundary, or session policy) always overrides any Allow. This is the 'explicit deny wins' principle. The evaluation order does not matter - if any applicable policy contains an explicit Deny for the action, the request is denied regardless of other Allow statements.

questions.question: A developer wants to ensure all objects uploaded to an S3 bucket are encrypted. Which bucket configuration enforces encryption?
options: Configure a default encryption setting on the bucket and add a bucket policy that denies PutObject requests without encryption headers
Enable versioning on the bucket, which automatically enables encryption
Create an IAM policy that only allows encrypted uploads
Enable S3 Object Lock, which requires encryption
questions.explanation: Setting default encryption ensures any object uploaded without explicit encryption headers is automatically encrypted. Adding a bucket policy with a condition that denies s3:PutObject when 's3:x-amz-server-side-encryption' is absent enforces that all uploads must include encryption. As of January 2023, S3 applies SSE-S3 encryption by default to all new objects.

questions.question: A developer needs to implement API Gateway authorization that validates JWT tokens issued by a third-party identity provider (not Cognito). Which authorizer type should be used?
options: A Lambda authorizer (formerly custom authorizer) that validates the JWT token and returns an IAM policy
A Cognito User Pool authorizer configured with the third-party IdP's settings
IAM authorization with STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity
API keys with usage plans
questions.explanation: Lambda authorizers accept a token or request parameters, run custom validation logic (e.g., JWT verification against a third-party IdP's JWKS endpoint), and return an IAM policy document that API Gateway uses to authorize the request. Cognito User Pool authorizers only work with tokens issued by Amazon Cognito. For HTTP APIs (not REST APIs), you can also use a JWT authorizer that natively validates third-party tokens.

questions.question: A developer has a Lambda function that retrieves a secret from Secrets Manager on every invocation. This causes high latency and cost. What is the recommended optimization?
options: Cache the secret value in a variable outside the Lambda handler function, so it persists across warm invocations within the same execution environment
Store the secret in a Lambda environment variable instead of Secrets Manager
Use provisioned concurrency to pre-load secrets into every execution environment
Create a DynamoDB table to cache secrets with a TTL
questions.explanation: Variables declared outside the Lambda handler function persist across invocations within the same execution environment (warm starts). By caching the Secrets Manager response in a module-level variable, subsequent invocations skip the API call. Add a TTL check to refresh the cache periodically. AWS also provides the Lambda Parameters and Secrets Extension that handles caching automatically.

questions.question: A developer needs to implement user registration, login, and password reset for a web application. Which AWS service provides these features as a managed service?
options: Amazon Cognito User Pools
AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO)
Amazon Cognito Identity Pools
AWS Directory Service
questions.explanation: Amazon Cognito User Pools provide a fully managed user directory with sign-up, sign-in, password reset, MFA, and account verification features. User Pools issue JWT tokens (ID, access, and refresh tokens) for authentication. Cognito Identity Pools (Federated Identities) provide temporary AWS credentials for accessing AWS services, which is a different use case.

questions.question: A developer is using Amazon Cognito for authentication. After a user logs in, the application needs temporary AWS credentials to upload files to S3. Which Cognito component provides AWS credentials?
options: Amazon Cognito Identity Pools (Federated Identities) that exchange the User Pool token for temporary AWS credentials via STS
Amazon Cognito User Pools that include AWS credentials in the ID token
Amazon Cognito Sync that synchronizes AWS credentials across devices
Amazon Cognito User Pool groups that map directly to IAM roles
questions.explanation: Cognito Identity Pools exchange tokens from identity providers (including Cognito User Pools, Google, Facebook, SAML) for temporary AWS credentials via STS. These credentials are scoped to an IAM role associated with the identity pool. The flow is: User Pool authentication -> ID token -> Identity Pool -> temporary AWS credentials (access key, secret key, session token) -> S3 access.

questions.question: A developer is using KMS to encrypt data. The application needs to encrypt a 10 MB file. Why can't the developer call KMS Encrypt directly with the 10 MB file?
options: KMS Encrypt has a 4 KB plaintext limit; envelope encryption using GenerateDataKey must be used for larger data
KMS Encrypt has a 1 MB limit; the file must be split into chunks
KMS Encrypt only works with text data, not binary files
KMS Encrypt requires the data to be stored in S3 first
questions.explanation: The KMS Encrypt API can only encrypt up to 4,096 bytes (4 KB) of data directly. For larger data, you must use envelope encryption: call GenerateDataKey to obtain a plaintext data key, encrypt the large file locally using that data key, and store the encrypted data key alongside the ciphertext. This approach is both more efficient (avoids sending large data over the network to KMS) and works within KMS's API limits.

questions.question: A developer needs to pass a database password to a Lambda function at runtime. The password must not be visible in the function's configuration in the AWS Console. Which approach should be used?
options: Store the password in Secrets Manager and retrieve it in the function code using the Secrets Manager SDK
Set it as a plaintext Lambda environment variable
Include it in the Lambda deployment package as a configuration file
Pass it as a query parameter in the API Gateway request
questions.explanation: Storing the password in AWS Secrets Manager and retrieving it at runtime via the SDK keeps the credential out of the Lambda configuration and source code. Secrets Manager encrypts secrets with KMS, provides automatic rotation, and logs access via CloudTrail. Lambda environment variables are visible in the Console unless encrypted with a customer KMS key, and even then the variable name is visible.

questions.question: A developer's application assumes an IAM role using STS AssumeRole. The assumed role's session duration needs to be exactly 1 hour. Which parameter controls this?
options: DurationSeconds parameter in the AssumeRole API call, set to 3600
MaxSessionDuration setting on the IAM role, set to 1 hour
TokenLifetime parameter in the IAM role's trust policy
SessionTimeout in the STS service configuration
questions.explanation: The DurationSeconds parameter in the STS AssumeRole call specifies the duration of the temporary credentials. The default is 1 hour (3600 seconds). The value can range from 900 seconds (15 minutes) to the role's MaxSessionDuration setting (default 1 hour, configurable up to 12 hours). The MaxSessionDuration on the role sets the upper bound, while DurationSeconds sets the actual session length.

questions.question: A developer needs to ensure that an API Gateway REST API only accepts HTTPS requests and rejects HTTP. How does API Gateway handle this by default?
options: API Gateway only supports HTTPS endpoints by default; HTTP requests are rejected automatically with no additional configuration needed
The developer must configure a custom domain with an SSL certificate and redirect HTTP to HTTPS
The developer must add a resource policy that denies requests without TLS
The developer must enable the 'Require TLS' option in the API settings
questions.explanation: API Gateway's default execute-api endpoint (https://{id}.execute-api.{region}.amazonaws.com) only supports HTTPS. There is no HTTP endpoint option. All API Gateway endpoints enforce TLS 1.2 by default. Custom domain names also require an ACM certificate and only support HTTPS. This is a built-in security feature that requires no configuration.

questions.question: A developer needs to reference configuration values in Lambda functions that change infrequently, such as feature flags and endpoint URLs. The values do not require encryption. Which is the most cost-effective storage option?
options: SSM Parameter Store Standard parameters (String type), which are free for standard throughput
AWS Secrets Manager, which provides versioning and rotation
A DynamoDB table with on-demand capacity
Lambda environment variables updated via deployment
questions.explanation: SSM Parameter Store Standard parameters (String or StringList type) are free of charge, support up to 10,000 parameters, and up to 4 KB per parameter. They are ideal for non-sensitive configuration data like feature flags and URLs. Secrets Manager charges per secret and per API call, making it more expensive for non-sensitive data. Parameter Store also supports hierarchical organization (e.g., /myapp/prod/db-host).

questions.question: A developer needs to allow an EC2 instance to call AWS APIs without storing credentials on the instance. Which feature provides this capability?
options: An IAM instance profile that attaches an IAM role to the EC2 instance
A security group rule that allows API traffic
An IAM user with API access keys stored in ~/.aws/credentials
A VPC endpoint for each AWS service the instance needs to access
questions.explanation: IAM instance profiles are containers for IAM roles that allow EC2 instances to assume a role. When an instance has an instance profile, temporary credentials are automatically provided via the instance metadata service (IMDS) and are automatically rotated. The AWS SDK on the instance retrieves these credentials transparently. No long-term credentials need to be stored on the instance.

questions.question: A developer is building a Lambda function that needs to make HTTPS calls to an external API. The function runs in a VPC. Security requirements mandate that all outbound traffic to AWS services must not traverse the public internet. How should the developer configure AWS service access?
options: Create VPC Interface Endpoints (PrivateLink) for each AWS service the function needs to access
Configure a NAT Gateway in a public subnet for AWS service access
Attach an internet gateway to the VPC and update route tables
Use VPC peering with the AWS service VPCs
questions.explanation: VPC Interface Endpoints (powered by AWS PrivateLink) create private connections to AWS services without requiring internet access. Traffic between the VPC and the service stays on the Amazon network. For Lambda functions in a VPC that need to call services like Secrets Manager, STS, or DynamoDB, interface endpoints eliminate the need for a NAT Gateway while keeping traffic private. Note: S3 and DynamoDB also support Gateway Endpoints, which are free.

questions.question: A developer is using Cognito User Pools with MFA. The application needs to support both SMS-based MFA and TOTP (time-based one-time password) as second factors. Which Cognito MFA configuration supports both options simultaneously?
options: Set MFA to 'Optional' and enable both SMS and TOTP as MFA methods; users choose their preferred method during registration
Create two separate User Pools, one for SMS MFA and one for TOTP MFA
Set MFA to 'Required' with SMS only, then add a Lambda trigger for TOTP validation
MFA in Cognito only supports one method at a time; use a Lambda trigger for the second method
questions.explanation: Cognito User Pools support both SMS and TOTP (software token) MFA methods simultaneously. When MFA is set to 'Optional' or 'Required', you can enable both SMS and TOTP. Users can register a TOTP device via the AssociateSoftwareToken API. During sign-in, if both are configured, the user can choose which method to use. TOTP is recommended over SMS for better security.

questions.question: A developer stores an RDS database password in AWS Secrets Manager. The application code calls GetSecretValue on every function invocation. How can the developer reduce the number of API calls to Secrets Manager?
options: Use the AWS Parameters and Secrets Lambda Extension, which caches secrets locally within the Lambda execution environment
Store the secret in an environment variable instead of Secrets Manager
Use Secrets Manager's built-in caching, which is enabled by default
Increase the Lambda function's memory to improve caching
questions.explanation: The AWS Parameters and Secrets Lambda Extension runs as a Lambda layer and provides a local HTTP cache for secrets and parameters. The extension retrieves the secret from Secrets Manager on first request and serves subsequent requests from the local cache until the TTL expires (default 300 seconds). This reduces Secrets Manager API calls, latency, and cost without changing application architecture.

questions.question: A developer needs to restrict API Gateway access so only users from a specific IP range can invoke the API. Which feature provides IP-based access control?
options: API Gateway resource policy with an IP condition (aws:SourceIp) to allow or deny requests based on IP address
API Gateway usage plans with IP address filtering
WAF IP set rules attached to the API Gateway stage
Lambda authorizer that checks the client's IP address
questions.explanation: API Gateway resource policies use IAM policy syntax to control access to the API. The 'aws:SourceIp' condition key allows you to specify CIDR ranges that are allowed or denied. This is evaluated before any other authorization mechanism. WAF also provides IP-based filtering but is a separate service with additional cost. Resource policies are free and built into API Gateway.

questions.question: A developer needs to implement client-side encryption of data before writing to DynamoDB. The encryption keys must be managed by AWS KMS with key policies that only allow specific IAM roles to encrypt and decrypt. Which tool should the developer use?
options: The AWS Database Encryption SDK (formerly DynamoDB Encryption Client) with KMS as the key provider
Call KMS Encrypt/Decrypt directly on each attribute value before DynamoDB operations
Enable DynamoDB encryption at rest with a customer-managed KMS key
Use the AWS Encryption SDK with DynamoDB as the data store
questions.explanation: The AWS Database Encryption SDK (formerly DynamoDB Encryption Client) provides client-side encryption specifically designed for DynamoDB. It encrypts attribute values before they are sent to DynamoDB and decrypts them on read. It supports signing to detect unauthorized modifications. Using KMS as the cryptographic materials provider integrates key management with KMS policies. DynamoDB encryption at rest is server-side and does not protect data in transit to DynamoDB.

questions.question: A developer is writing an IAM policy and wants to follow the principle of least privilege. Which approach best implements this principle?
options: Grant only the specific actions needed on the specific resources required, using explicit resource ARNs instead of wildcards
Grant full access to the needed services (e.g., s3:*) and restrict at the network level
Start with AdministratorAccess and remove permissions as issues arise
Grant ReadOnlyAccess to all services and add write permissions as needed
questions.explanation: Least privilege means granting only the permissions necessary to perform a task. This involves specifying exact actions (e.g., s3:GetObject instead of s3:*) and exact resources (e.g., arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/* instead of *). IAM Access Analyzer can help identify unused permissions and generate least-privilege policies based on actual access patterns.

questions.question: A developer discovers that a Lambda function's environment variable containing a database password is visible in the AWS Console in plaintext. How can the developer prevent this?
options: Enable encryption helpers in the Lambda console, which encrypts the environment variable with a KMS key; the function code must decrypt it at runtime
Lambda environment variables cannot be encrypted; use Secrets Manager instead
Set the environment variable's visibility to 'hidden' in the function configuration
Use a Lambda Layer to store the credential, which hides it from the console
questions.explanation: Lambda supports encrypting environment variables at rest with the default Lambda service key AND in transit with a customer KMS key using 'encryption helpers'. When encryption helpers are enabled, values are encrypted before being sent to Lambda and must be decrypted in the function code using the KMS SDK. This ensures the values are never visible in plaintext in the Console. However, using Secrets Manager is generally preferred for credential management.

questions.question: A developer is building a microservices application where Service A needs to securely call Service B's API Gateway endpoint. Both services are in the same AWS account. The developer wants to avoid API keys and use IAM-based authentication. Which approach should be used?
options: Enable IAM authorization on the API Gateway method and have Service A sign requests using SigV4 with its IAM role credentials
Use a shared Cognito User Pool token between the services
Create a mutual TLS (mTLS) configuration on the API Gateway
Use API Gateway resource policies to whitelist Service A's IAM role
questions.explanation: IAM authorization on API Gateway requires requests to be signed with AWS Signature Version 4 (SigV4). Service A uses its IAM role's credentials to sign each request. API Gateway validates the signature and checks the caller's IAM policies. This provides strong authentication without managing API keys or tokens. The AWS SDK can handle SigV4 signing automatically.

questions.question: A developer needs to create a Lambda function that can only be invoked by a specific S3 bucket. Which type of policy grants this permission?
options: A resource-based policy on the Lambda function that grants s3.amazonaws.com permission to invoke the function, with a condition on the source ARN matching the specific bucket
An IAM identity policy attached to the Lambda execution role
A VPC endpoint policy restricting Lambda invocation sources
An S3 bucket policy granting lambda:InvokeFunction permission
questions.explanation: Lambda resource-based policies (also called function policies) control which AWS services and accounts can invoke the function. To allow S3 to invoke Lambda, add a resource-based policy with Principal: s3.amazonaws.com and a condition on aws:SourceArn matching the S3 bucket ARN. This is required in addition to the S3 event notification configuration. Without this policy, S3 cannot invoke the Lambda function.

questions.question: A developer needs to ensure that data transmitted between an application and a DynamoDB table is encrypted in transit. What should the developer configure?
options: No configuration is needed; DynamoDB endpoints use HTTPS (TLS) by default for all API calls
Enable the 'EncryptInTransit' option in the DynamoDB table settings
Create a VPC endpoint for DynamoDB with TLS enforcement
Use the DynamoDB Encryption Client for in-transit encryption
questions.explanation: All DynamoDB API calls use HTTPS (TLS 1.2) by default. The AWS SDK automatically connects to DynamoDB's HTTPS endpoints, encrypting all data in transit. No additional configuration is required. DynamoDB does not offer unencrypted HTTP endpoints. The DynamoDB Encryption Client provides additional client-side encryption of the data itself, which is different from transport-level encryption.

questions.question: A developer is building a Lambda function that accesses an RDS database. Instead of managing database passwords, the developer wants to use short-lived authentication tokens. Which approach should be used?
options: Enable IAM database authentication on the RDS instance and generate an authentication token using the RDS SDK
Create a Cognito User Pool and use the Cognito tokens for database authentication
Use AWS STS to generate temporary database credentials
Configure the RDS instance to accept Lambda execution role ARNs as usernames
questions.explanation: IAM database authentication for RDS allows using IAM credentials instead of database passwords. The application generates a temporary authentication token by calling the RDS API with IAM credentials. The token is valid for 15 minutes and serves as the database password. This eliminates the need to store database passwords. The Lambda function's execution role must have the rds-db:connect permission.

questions.question: A developer has a Lambda function with an execution role that has a permissions boundary attached. The execution role allows s3:PutObject on all buckets, but the permissions boundary only allows s3:PutObject on 'arn:aws:s3:::approved-bucket/*'. What is the effective permission?
options: s3:PutObject only on 'arn:aws:s3:::approved-bucket/*' because the effective permissions are the intersection of the role policy and the permissions boundary
s3:PutObject on all buckets because the role policy overrides the boundary
s3:PutObject is denied on all buckets because the boundary restricts the role
The request will fail with an access denied error for all buckets
questions.explanation: Permissions boundaries set the maximum permissions an IAM entity can have. The effective permissions are the INTERSECTION of the identity-based policy and the permissions boundary. If the role allows s3:PutObject on all buckets but the boundary only allows it on 'approved-bucket', the effective permission is limited to 'approved-bucket'. Permissions boundaries cannot grant permissions; they only limit them.

questions.question: A developer needs to rotate an RDS database password stored in Secrets Manager. Which feature handles rotation automatically without modifying application code?
options: Secrets Manager automatic rotation with a built-in Lambda rotation function for RDS
A CloudWatch Events rule that triggers a custom Lambda function to change the password
RDS automatic password rotation in the database engine settings
IAM credential rotation applied to the database user
questions.explanation: Secrets Manager provides built-in Lambda rotation functions for RDS, Redshift, and DocumentDB. When automatic rotation is enabled, Secrets Manager invokes the rotation function on a schedule (e.g., every 30 days) to generate a new password, update the database, and store the new secret. Applications using Secrets Manager API automatically get the new credentials. No application code changes are needed.

questions.question: A developer is implementing API Gateway with a Cognito User Pool authorizer. When a user makes a request, which token must be included in the Authorization header?
options: The ID token or Access token from the Cognito User Pool authentication response
The Refresh token from the Cognito authentication response
An API key generated by API Gateway
An AWS STS session token
questions.explanation: When using a Cognito User Pool authorizer with API Gateway, the client includes the ID token or Access token in the Authorization header. The ID token contains user identity claims (name, email, groups), while the Access token contains scope and authorization information. API Gateway validates the token signature and expiration against the Cognito User Pool. The Refresh token is used only to obtain new ID/Access tokens.

questions.question: A developer is implementing a Lambda function that processes sensitive PII data. The data must be encrypted in transit and at rest, and access to the encryption key must be audited. Which comprehensive approach meets all requirements?
options: Use a customer-managed KMS key for Lambda environment variable encryption, enable KMS key CloudTrail logging, and ensure the function communicates with services via HTTPS endpoints only
Use the default AWS-managed Lambda encryption key and enable CloudWatch Logs encryption
Store PII in DynamoDB with default encryption and enable VPC Flow Logs
Use client-side encryption with a locally-generated key stored in the function code
questions.explanation: A customer-managed KMS key provides full control over key policies and enables detailed CloudTrail logging of all key usage (who decrypted what, when). Lambda encrypts environment variables at rest, and all AWS service endpoints use HTTPS for transit encryption. The customer-managed key allows auditing key access through CloudTrail, meeting the audit requirement. AWS-managed keys do not provide the same level of audit control.

questions.question: A developer is configuring CORS on an API Gateway REST API. The frontend application is hosted on a different domain. Which HTTP method must the API support for CORS preflight requests?
options: OPTIONS
HEAD
TRACE
CONNECT
questions.explanation: CORS preflight requests use the HTTP OPTIONS method. When a browser sends a cross-origin request with certain characteristics (custom headers, non-simple methods), it first sends an OPTIONS request to check if the actual request is allowed. API Gateway can be configured to return the appropriate CORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, Access-Control-Allow-Headers) in the OPTIONS response.

questions.question: A developer is building a Lambda function that needs to access secrets from both AWS Secrets Manager and SSM Parameter Store. Rather than making API calls to both services, what single optimization can handle both?
options: Deploy the AWS Parameters and Secrets Lambda Extension, which provides a local cache for both Secrets Manager and Parameter Store values
Use a single DynamoDB table to store all secrets and parameters
Store all values in Secrets Manager, which also supports parameter-style lookups
Use Lambda environment variables for all configuration and secret values
questions.explanation: The AWS Parameters and Secrets Lambda Extension is a Lambda layer that caches values from both Secrets Manager and SSM Parameter Store. It exposes a local HTTP endpoint (localhost:2773) that the function calls instead of making SDK calls to the actual services. This reduces latency, API costs, and the number of network calls.
